name: Quantum Risk
technicalDefinition: The risk that future quantum computers will compromise currently deployed cryptographic systems.
plainEnglish: Future computers may eventually unlock data that appears secure today.
whyItMatters: Sensitive information can remain valuable for many years, so data stolen now may still create harm later.
realWorldExample: Customer records stolen today may be decrypted in the future if they were protected with vulnerable cryptography.

name: Quantum Risk Readiness
technicalDefinition: A management practice for quantum risk that clarifies scope, ownership, expected evidence, and business impact.
plainEnglish: This turns a technical topic into an accountable management action with owners, dates, evidence, and trade-offs.
whyItMatters: Quantum change can alter long-term security, investment choices, and technical dependencies. If Quantum Risk Readiness is misunderstood, leaders may approve the wrong risk, underfund the work, or miss a critical dependency.
realWorldExample: A leadership team reviews which high-value records could still matter when stronger quantum computers arrive. A specific quantum risk readiness is used to turn the finding into owners, dates, evidence, and follow-up action.

plainEnglish: In plain English, this means keeping critical services running or restoring them quickly when disruption happens. For leaders, Service Dependency makes the issue in a way leaders can see, compare, and act on easier to understand and manage.
whyItMatters: Resilience determines whether a disruption becomes a brief interruption or a major business crisis. If Service Dependency is misunderstood, leaders may approve the wrong risk, underfund the work, or miss a critical dependency.
realWorldExample: A payments platform proves it can restore transactions from a clean backup after ransomware. The discussion is recorded as Service Dependency so technical detail can be translated into a business decision.

name: Service Dependency Scenario
technicalDefinition: A management practice for service dependency that clarifies scope, ownership, expected evidence, and business impact.
plainEnglish: This turns a technical topic into an accountable management action with owners, dates, evidence, and trade-offs.
whyItMatters: Resilience determines whether a disruption becomes a brief interruption or a major business crisis. If Service Dependency Scenario is misunderstood, leaders may approve the wrong risk, underfund the work, or miss a critical dependency.
realWorldExample: A payments platform proves it can restore transactions from a clean backup after ransomware. A specific service dependency scenario is used to turn the finding into owners, dates, evidence, and follow-up action.

name: Digital Transformation

plainEnglish: In plain English, this means checking that a decision, control, model, or system meets the need it was built for. For leaders, Cloud Validation makes the proof that something works as intended easier to understand and manage.
whyItMatters: Validation prevents teams from relying on assumptions that have not been proven. If Cloud Validation is misunderstood, leaders may approve the wrong risk, underfund the work, or miss a critical dependency.
realWorldExample: A closure pack is validated before an audit issue is marked complete. The discussion is recorded as Cloud Validation so technical detail can be translated into a business decision.

name: Cloud Validation Criteria
technicalDefinition: A management practice for cloud validation that clarifies scope, ownership, expected evidence, and business impact.
plainEnglish: This turns a technical topic into an accountable management action with owners, dates, evidence, and trade-offs.
whyItMatters: Validation prevents teams from relying on assumptions that have not been proven. If Cloud Validation Criteria is misunderstood, leaders may approve the wrong risk, underfund the work, or miss a critical dependency.
realWorldExample: A closure pack is validated before an audit issue is marked complete. A specific cloud validation criteria is used to turn the finding into owners, dates, evidence, and follow-up action.
